primary_patch.before:
4.2.2 What the globe predicts

Standard ionospheric physics (the Chapman–Sq mechanism, known since 1933) already predicts magnetic dips of 5–25 nT during solar eclipses from reduced ionospheric conductivity. This has been measured and confirmed across dozens of eclipses over 90 years.

Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first identified by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the Moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-layer conductivity (at 90–150 km altitude) and disrupting the solar quiet (Sq) current system. This produces measurable ground-level magnetic perturbations that track the eclipse shadow geometry. Modern implementations of the Chapman mechanism — particularly the Ashour-Chapman models refined by Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) — match observed eclipse magnetic signals "strikingly accurately" at INTERMAGNET stations, predicting magnitudes of 5–25 nT depending on eclipse coverage, station latitude, local ionospheric conditions, and Sq current geometry. The literature is extensive: 207 observations across 39 eclipses (1991–2016) and a multi-station INTERMAGNET study of 4 total eclipses confirm the mechanism quantitatively.

The dome's site characterizes the globe prediction as having "no physical mechanism" for eclipse magnetic effects. In fact, the Chapman Sq-current mechanism has been refined for over 90 years and quantitatively predicts both the magnitude and the shadow-tracking geometry of eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations.
primary_patch.after:
4.2.2 What the globe predicts

The dome's eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. The dome's published falsification criterion treats “globe predicts 0 nT” as the test; standard ionospheric physics (Chapman 1933) actually predicts 5–25 nT, confirmed across dozens of eclipses over nearly a century, and one of the dome's own baseline inputs already sits outside its ±3 nT “globe” window.

The dome's eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong. From the dome's live-power dashboard, Domain 11 (archived 2026-04-07; verified live at john09289.github.io/predictions/live.html on 2026-04-26):

Eclipse 2026 — Fails if measured anomaly is within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction).

And the dome's eclipse countdown banner repeats the same framing more directly:

X days until Aug 12, 2026 eclipse — ECM predicts -[value] nT, globe predicts 0 nT. Requires Kp < 2.

The dome treats zero, not five-to-twenty-five, as the standard-physics expectation. This is the load-bearing error that lets the hedging structure (see §4.2.3) read like a discrimination test in the first place: any non-zero measurement at the prediction stations becomes “confirmation of ECM, falsification of globe” — but only because the globe's actual prediction has been replaced with a strawman. What standard physics actually predicts is enough to overlap nearly all of the dome's prediction window before any data is collected.

Eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations were first identified by Chapman (1933), who proposed that the Moon's shadow reduces ionospheric UV ionization, lowering E-layer conductivity (at 90–150 km altitude) and disrupting the solar quiet (Sq) current system. This produces measurable ground-level magnetic perturbations that track the eclipse shadow geometry. Modern implementations of the Chapman mechanism — particularly the Ashour-Chapman models refined by Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) — match observed eclipse magnetic signals “strikingly accurately” at INTERMAGNET stations, predicting magnitudes of 5–25 nT depending on eclipse coverage, station latitude, local ionospheric conditions, and Sq current geometry. The literature is extensive: 207 observations across 39 eclipses (1991–2016) and a multi-station INTERMAGNET study of 4 total eclipses confirm the mechanism quantitatively.

The dome's site separately characterizes the globe prediction as having “no physical mechanism” for eclipse magnetic effects. The Chapman Sq-current mechanism has been refined for over 90 years and quantitatively predicts both the magnitude and the shadow-tracking geometry of eclipse-induced magnetic perturbations.

By the dome's own published criterion, the dome has already failed. The dome's eclipse formula uses a 2017 Boulder (BOU) observation of −10.9 nT as one of its baselines (see §4.2.5 for the full input chain). The BOU 2017 figure is itself a Chapman-mechanism measurement on a spherical Earth — the dome is, in effect, treating a globe-physics datum as input to a “globe predicts 0” prediction. By the dome's own falsification rule (“within ±3 nT of 0”), −10.9 nT lies outside the window. The dome must either accept its own falsification criterion (and concede the “globe prediction” it asserts is wrong, since one of its own baseline inputs is roughly 3.6× outside the ±3 nT envelope) or revise the criterion (and concede the prediction was never discriminating in the first place).
primary_patch.rationale: Wholesale section rewrite per HNOTE-OPERATOR-4-2-2-MISSTATEMENT-LEAD-001. Five-step structure exactly as commissioned: (1) lead sentence "the dome's eclipse argument depends on a specific claim ... and that claim is wrong"; (2) verbatim quote of the dome's falsification criterion (Domain 11 live-power); (3) note this framing is what makes the prediction LOOK discriminating, with cross-link forward to §4.2.3; (4) state what standard physics actually predicts — Chapman 1933, 5–25 nT, dozens of eclipses, ~90 years (the existing content takes over); (5) close with the BOU-2017-already-falsifies hammer linking to §4.2.5. The existing Chapman content (paragraphs 1 and 2 of the prior body) is preserved verbatim as supporting prose, demoted from lead.
primary_patch.verification_note: Substring uniqueness: the entire <details id="p4-422">…</details> block exists exactly once in part4.html (verified by node search). The HTML literal in `before` was extracted from a live read of the clone's data/sections.json on 2026-04-26 and is byte-identical to the source. The replacement preserves the existing <details id>, <summary class="ps-summary">, ps-tldr structure, and <div class="ps-detail"> wrapper (Progressive Disclosure compliance per analyst-mode1-expansions.md).

secondary_patches.before: The dual-baseline design creates a prediction window that covers most physically plausible outcomes for a quiet-day eclipse.
secondary_patches.after: The dual-baseline design only reads as a “discrimination test” because (per §4.2.2) the dome misstates the globe prediction as zero; with the actual Chapman range (5–25 nT) restored, the dome's window overlaps the globe's expectation rather than excluding it. The dual-baseline design creates a prediction window that covers most physically plausible outcomes for a quiet-day eclipse.
secondary_patches.rationale: Per HNOTE INTEGRATION NOTES: "Section 4.2.3 (hedging structure) needs a one-sentence cross-reference to 4.2.2's misstatement so the reader sees how 'globe predicts 0' enables the hedging." This adds a single leading sentence to §4.2.3's opening paragraph that names the dependency explicitly. The prior opening sentence is preserved verbatim immediately after.

tldr_change: TLDR rewritten from a Chapman-only summary ("standard physics predicts 5–25 nT, measured for 90 years") to a misstatement-led summary ("the dome's eclipse argument depends on a specific claim about what standard physics predicts — and that claim is wrong"). The new TLDR (a) names the load-bearing error in the lead clause, (b) contrasts the dome's "0 nT globe prediction" with Chapman's actual 5–25 nT, and (c) notes that one of the dome's own baseline inputs already sits outside its ±3 nT "globe" window — the hammer that closes the section body. Length: ~85 words (within the 2–3 sentence ps-tldr norm).

holes_addressed.description: Prior §4.2.2 established what standard physics predicts but did not establish that the dome explicitly misstates standard physics. A reader landing on §4.2.2 saw "globe predicts 5–25 nT" but had to wait until §4.2.3 to learn that the dome's discrimination test depends on "globe predicts 0". The load-bearing error sat unargued for an entire section.
holes_addressed.fix: New lead paragraph quotes the dome's own falsification criterion verbatim (live-power Domain 11) and the eclipse countdown banner. Reader sees the misstatement in the dome's own words at the top of the section, before the Chapman explanation.

holes_addressed.hole: Missing cross-reference from §4.2.3 to the misstatement
holes_addressed.description: Prior §4.2.3 ("hedging structure") explained why the dual-baseline design creates a wide window, but did not name the dependency on the prior section's misstatement. A reader who skipped §4.2.2 saw the hedging structure as a self-contained critique rather than a structural consequence of the misstatement.
holes_addressed.fix: Single new sentence at the start of §4.2.3 names the dependency: "The dual-baseline design only reads as a discrimination test because (per §4.2.2) the dome misstates the globe prediction as zero."

holes_addressed.description: The dome's own ±3 nT falsification rule is satisfied (in the failing direction) by one of the dome's own baseline inputs (−10.9 nT BOU 2017). Prior §4.2.5 noted that the BOU 2017 figure is a Chapman-mechanism measurement, but did not pivot to the closing-the-trap argument: by the dome's rule, the dome has already failed.
holes_addressed.fix: New closing paragraph in §4.2.2 makes the argument explicit, links to §4.2.5 for the input chain, and frames the dome's choice as a trilemma (accept own criterion → concede globe right; revise criterion → concede prediction never discriminating).

new_evidence_added: Verbatim quote of dome's live-power Domain 11 falsification criterion (archived 2026-04-07; verified live 2026-04-26).
Verbatim quote of dome's eclipse countdown banner template (same archive + live verification).
Arithmetic note that |−10.9| / 3.0 = 3.6× outside the ±3 nT window (computed inline; not previously stated this way).

anticipated_objections.objection: The dome could argue that "globe predicts 0 nT" in the live-power banner is shorthand for "globe predicts 0 nT in the V51 fit", not "0 nT under standard ionospheric physics" — that is, it's the residual after the dome's own subtraction, not a claim about Chapman.
anticipated_objections.response: The Domain 11 falsification criterion ("Measured anomaly within ±3 nT of 0 (globe prediction)") explicitly labels 0 nT as the "globe prediction". The dome's own falsification rule treats 0 nT as the standard-physics expectation; this is not a shorthand artifact, it is the registered test. If the dome retreats to "0 nT was just our shorthand", it concedes the falsification rule was never operational in the form published — which is itself the argument we are making in the closing paragraph (revise the criterion → concede the prediction was never discriminating).

anticipated_objections.objection: BOU 2017 was measured during a different eclipse (2017 Aug 21) under different geomagnetic conditions. The dome's ±3 nT rule applies to the 2026 measurement, not retroactively to historical baselines.
anticipated_objections.response: The dome's own eclipse formula uses the 2017 BOU observation as a numerical input to the 2026 prediction (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF; baseline ≈ BOU 2017). Whether the rule is intended to apply retroactively or not, the dome is feeding a value that violates its own rule into the prediction it is testing. The closing paragraph deliberately frames this as a trilemma (accept the rule → globe-prediction is wrong; revise the rule → prediction never discriminating); the dome cannot route around the trilemma by pleading that 2017 was "different conditions" without conceding that the rule's domain of applicability was not specified in advance.

anticipated_objections.objection: Quoting the live-power page is fragile — the dome edits this page periodically. If the quote is removed before integration, the lead falls apart.
anticipated_objections.response: Two defenses are in place: (1) the archived form (raw-text/08-live-power.txt, committed 2026-04-09, header-dated 2026-04-07) is preserved in-repo as a permanent record; (2) the curmudgeon review step explicitly re-verifies the quote at review time, and the live_quote_verification block in this EXP includes a drift_note instructing curmudgeon to switch to the archive form and add an edit-date if the live page changes. The argument structure ("dome misstated → dome's own input falsifies") survives the dome editing the banner because the published-and-archived form is the citable artifact.

anticipated_objections.objection: Adding a misstatement framing risks reading as "you misspoke" rather than "your prediction is unfalsifiable in the form published". Operator decisions depend on tone.
anticipated_objections.response: The lead deliberately uses "and that claim is wrong" rather than "misspoke". The closing paragraph makes the structural point explicit (the dome must accept the rule and concede globe-correct, or revise the rule and concede non-discriminating). Tone-wise: the section calls out an error in the dome's published material with a verbatim quote; calling it anything softer than "wrong" would obscure what the dome's falsification criterion actually says.

anticipated_objections.objection: The TLDR is now ~85 words / three full sentences, longer than the prior ~38-word two-sentence TLDR.
anticipated_objections.response: TLDR length is the right operating range per CLAUDE.md "Progressive Disclosure" guidance ("2–3 sentences") and matches the EXP-205 §8b.3 TLDR length (~80 words). The new TLDR is three sentences, each load-bearing: (a) names the misstatement, (b) contrasts dome's 0 with Chapman's 5–25, (c) notes BOU 2017 already falsifies. Cutting any of the three drops the punchline.

kernel_of_truth: The dome got two things substantially right that the new lead does not undermine. (1) An eclipse magnetic anomaly is a real, measurable phenomenon — not a fabrication. Chapman 1933 + 90 years of measurements make this point in the dome's favor. (2) The dome's prediction does include a physical-mechanism placeholder (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF), with the FSF derived from H(r)/r at Pearson r ≈ 0.9999 across nine stations (per WIN-010 post-EXP-220 / EXP-228 concessions). What the dome got wrong is the description of what standard physics predicts: "globe predicts 0 nT" is not a defensible characterization of Chapman. The new lead does not deny the eclipse-magnetic phenomenon, does not deny the dome's formula structure, and does not deny the FSF ratio derivation. It denies only that the dome's "globe prediction" represents standard physics. That denial is supported by the dome's own published falsification criterion in juxtaposition with one of its own baseline inputs.
why_kernel_doesnt_save_the_dome: The dome's formula structure (Δ = baseline × coverage × FSF) is real, and the FSF ratio derivation across nine stations is real, but the absolute magnitude is observation-anchored to BOU 2017 (a Chapman-mechanism measurement on a spherical Earth) — so the prediction is not first-principles dome-derived. And the discriminating criterion ("globe predicts 0") is not a defensible representation of standard physics — Chapman's 5–25 nT range encompasses the dome's entire baseline-times-coverage range and one of the dome's own input values. The dome's "globe-vs-dome" test is structurally a test of one Chapman-derived datum against another, with the discrimination provided only by misstating the globe expectation as zero.

§8b.3 (post-EXP-205) — uses the same "magnitude-only Pass band does not distinguish dome from globe" framing; §4.2.2 misstatement-lead is the upstream cause

newsworthy_rationale: The argument is a sharpening of an existing critique, not a novel finding. The dome's misstatement of standard physics has been implicitly load-bearing in §4.2.3 / §4.2.5 from prior expansions; this EXP makes it explicit and adds the BOU-already-falsifies hammer. None of that is a "compelling headline" event — it's structural prose tightening per operator commission.
other_side_of_the_argument: A defender of the dome could argue (i) the "globe predicts 0" framing is reader-shorthand for "globe predicts no eclipse-specific anomaly above background", which is not the same as "Chapman predicts 0", and that we are uncharitably reading the dome's shorthand as a literal claim about Chapman. The response in the closing trilemma covers this: if the dome retreats to "we meant something else by 0", it concedes the rule was not operational in the published form — exactly the second horn we already give them. (ii) A defender could argue the BOU-2017-already-falsifies hammer is a domain-of-applicability error, since the rule was registered for the 2026 measurement and the BOU 2017 figure is a historical baseline. Response: same trilemma — if the rule's domain was unspecified in advance, the dome concedes the rule was not operational; if it was specified to apply only to 2026, the dome owes a published artifact showing that pre-registration. Neither defense lifts the dome out of the trilemma; they choose which horn to take.
build_impact: After patch application, run `node build.js html` to regenerate docs/index.html (part4.html → rendered HTML via build-scripts/generate-html.js). Then run tests/run.sh to verify no regression on existing assertions for §4.2.2 and §4.2.3. No data values change; no test should newly fail.
